definition: Services CPI inflation remains sticky at 3.5-4.0% driven by unit labor cost growth of 3.0-4.0%, with a historical base rate of 0/5 for resolution to ≤3% without recession or productivity revolution in post-1960 episodes.
evidence: 0/5 base rate for services CPI returning to ≤3% without recession in post-1960 episodes
Services unit labor cost growth at 3.0-4.0% fully explains persistence via NKPC with Calvo pricing
Services ex-shelter at ~4-5% is the binding constraint on the inflation outlook
The two partial successes (1991, 2008) both required recessions opening significant output gaps
Services CPI at 3.5-4.0% with unit labor cost support of 3.0-4.0%
0/5 base rate for resolution without recession (1/7 including 1994-95 productivity-assisted success)
Clopper-Pearson 95% CI is [0%, 52.2%] — statistically honest framing
5 episodes are not i.i.d. draws from a stationary process
open_questions: Can monetary policy resolve services inflation without recession given impaired transmission?
What is the relative contribution of labor costs vs. transmission impairment vs. credit market structure?
How much of services persistence is structural vs. cyclical?
Can immigration surge of 3-4M workers provide sufficient labor supply relief?
Can sectoral AI deployment create disinflationary pressure in CPI-relevant services categories?
Are there novel disinflationary channels with no analogue in the n=5 reference set?
What is the CPI weight of sub-categories where AI deployment is already live?
